title: How to Create a Financial Practice That Honors Your Whole Self
body:
How to Create a Financial Practice That Honors Your Whole Self

You're probably lying to yourself about money. Not intentionally, not maliciously, but it's happening. You tell yourself you're fine, you've got it handled, or you'll deal with it later. Meanwhile, a low hum of anxiety lives in your gut, a tightness in your chest whenever the bills come, a quiet shame you don't dare voice. This isn't about budgeting. This isn't about investment strategies. This is about the deep, often unspoken, relationship you have with money, and how that relationship is silently shaping your entire life. It's time to stop pretending. It's time to build a financial practice that actually honors who you are, not who you think you should be.

The Unseen Weight of Money Shame

Honestly, most financial advice misses the point. It treats money as a purely logical construct, a series of numbers and spreadsheets. But you and I know it's not. Money is deeply emotional. It's tied to our survival, our security, our self-worth. It's soaked in family history, societal expectations, and personal trauma. You feel that? You carry it all, whether you realize it or not.

Think about it. When someone asks about your salary, do you feel a pang of judgment - either that it's too little or, sometimes, too much? When you look at your bank balance, is there a sense of peace or a tightening knot? Notice what your body does when you think about your debt. Is there a clench in your jaw? A shallow breath? A slump in your shoulders? These aren't just thoughts; they're somatic responses, signals from your nervous system that this topic isn't neutral for you.

Barbara Huson, in her work on women and wealth, talks extensively about how societal conditioning often leads us to under-earn, to feel undeserving of true financial abundance. It's not just a lack of skills; it's a deep-seated belief system. Lynne Twist, author of The Soul of Money (paid link), goes even further, showing how our relationship with money often mirrors our relationship with life itself - scarcity, sufficiency, or abundance. Most of us are stuck in scarcity, even when we have more than enough. The poverty consciousness doesn't care how much you earn. It'll find you, even in a mansion.

This isn't about blaming anyone. It's about acknowledging the truth. You're not broken for feeling this way. You're human. And the first step toward a healthier financial practice isn't another budget app; it's radical honesty with yourself about the emotional baggage you're carrying. Right?!

Your Bank Account is a Mirror, Not a Scorecard

Here's the thing. We often treat our bank account balance like a report card, a definitive judgment on our worth. High balance, A+. Low balance, F. But that's a lie. Your bank account is a mirror, not a scorecard. It reflects your habits, your beliefs, your fears, your priorities. It shows you where you've been investing your energy, consciously or unconsciously. It's not judging you; it's showing you your present reality, which is always an invitation to change.

If you look in a mirror and don't like what you see, you don't smash the mirror. You change something about yourself. It's the same with your money. If your finances feel chaotic, if debt is piling up, if you're constantly stressed, that's not a moral failing. That's data. It's information. It's telling you something needs to shift, not just in your external actions, but in your internal world.

George Kinder, the father of the Life Planning movement, understood this implicitly. He didn't start with numbers; he started with life. He asked people about their deepest values, their dreams, their fears. Only after understanding the whole person did he bring in the financial tools. Because what's the point of having all the money in the world if it doesn't align with who you truly are and what you truly want? What's the point of a perfect budget if it leaves you feeling constricted and resentful?

This whole self approach means acknowledging that you're not just a mind or a body, but a complex interplay of emotions, spirit, history, and aspirations. Your financial life needs to honor all of that, not just the logical, calculating part. Know what I mean?

Healing Your Money Trauma: The Somatic Approach

For many of us, our relationship with money is deeply intertwined with trauma, big or small. Maybe you grew up in poverty and the fear of not having enough is etched into your nervous system. Maybe you witnessed constant arguments about money, leading you to associate it with conflict. Maybe you experienced financial betrayal, making trust impossible. These aren't just memories; they're embodied experiences.

As Bessel van der Kolk so powerfully articulates in The Body Keeps the Score (paid link), trauma isn't just in your head. It's in your cells, in your breath, in your posture. So, if your money story is painful, simply "thinking positive" or "making a budget" isn't going to cut it. You have to address the somatic component.

This means cultivating awareness of your physical sensations when you engage with money. When you pay bills, do you hold your breath? When you check your balance, does your stomach clench? When you think about saving, do your shoulders tense up? These aren't just random reactions. They're your body's way of telling you that old wounds are being triggered. Instead of pushing them away, can you lean into them with compassion? Can you simply observe them without judgment?

A simple practice: before opening your banking app or looking at a bill, take three deep breaths. Place a hand on your heart, another on your belly. Acknowledge any fear or anxiety that arises. Say to yourself, "I'm feeling fear around money right now. That's okay. I'm safe to feel this." This isn't about making the fear disappear. It's about creating a container for it, so it doesn't overwhelm you. It's about differentiating the past pain from the present moment. You're not five years old anymore, powerless to the financial chaos around you. You're an adult now, capable of making different choices.

Conscious Spending: Beyond the Budget

Traditional budgeting often feels like deprivation, like a punishment. It's about cutting, restricting, saying "no." No wonder it rarely sticks. A conscious spending practice, however, is about alignment. It's about asking, "Does this expenditure truly serve my deepest values? Does it bring me closer to who I want to be and the life I want to live?"

This isn't about being perfect. It's about being intentional. Before you buy something, pause. Ask yourself:

  • Am I buying this out of true need or desire, or out of impulse, boredom, or a need for external validation?
  • Does this purchase align with my core values? If one of your values is freedom, does this purchase create more freedom or more obligation?
  • How will I feel about this purchase in an hour? A day? A week? A month?
  • Is there a more aligned way to spend this money?

This isn't about judgment. It's about self-inquiry. It's about bringing awareness to an activity that's often unconscious. Many people spend their lives chasing external markers of success, buying things they don't truly need, to impress people they don't truly like, all while feeling an emptiness inside. Krishnamurti often spoke about the futility of seeking external gratification to fill an internal void. Money, when used unconsciously, becomes another one of those external distractions.

Consider the work of Bari Tessler, author of The Art of Money (paid link). She advocates for "money dates" - regular, gentle check-ins with your finances that aren't about judgment but about curiosity and connection. It's about listening to your money story, not just dictating it. It's about understanding your patterns, not just trying to force new ones. It's a practice of self-care, not self-flagellation. Does that land?

Building a Practice of Financial Self-Care

Just like you wouldn't expect to get fit by going to the gym once a year, you can't expect your financial life to transform with sporadic, panicked interventions. This requires a consistent, gentle practice. Here are some elements to consider:

Weekly Money Check-in

This isn't a deep dive. It's a 15-30 minute appointment with yourself and your money. Sit down with a cup of tea. Light a candle. Make it sacred. Open your banking apps, look at your credit card statements, glance at your investments. Don't judge. Just observe. What's the flow? What's coming in? What's going out? Are there any surprises? This helps you stay informed and reduces anxiety, because the unknown is always scarier than the known.

Daily Gratitude for Money

Even if you're struggling, find something to be grateful for. The roof over your head, the food on your table, the clothes on your back, the internet connection you're using to read this. Every single one of these things required money to come into being. Acknowledge it. Thank it. This shifts your vibration from lack to appreciation, which is a powerful energetic magnet. The universe responds to gratitude, not to constant complaint.

Conscious Giving

Giving isn't just for the wealthy. It's a powerful act of abundance, regardless of your financial situation. Even a small amount, given with intention and an open heart, signals to yourself and the universe that you have more than enough. It breaks the scarcity mindset. It reminds you that you are a conduit, not just a container.

Boundary Setting

This is huge. Money is often a source of boundary violations - from family asking for loans you can't afford, to friends pressuring you to spend beyond your means, to advertisers constantly telling you you're not enough. Learn to say "no" - to others and to yourself. Protect your financial energy. This is a radical act of self-love.

Journaling Your Money Story

Write about your earliest money memories. What did your parents teach you about money, implicitly and explicitly? What are your biggest fears about money? Your biggest dreams? What does money represent to you? Freedom? Security? Power? Love? Shame? Getting these thoughts out of your head and onto paper can be incredibly clarifying. It's like shining a flashlight into the dark corners of your subconscious.

This isn't about becoming a financial guru overnight. It's about slowly, gently, consistently building a relationship with money that is founded on awareness, self-compassion, and truth. It's about integrating your financial life into your spiritual practice, recognizing that how you manage your resources is a reflection of your consciousness. You're not just managing dollars and cents; you're managing your life force, your energy, your potential. When you treat money as sacred, it begins to act that way in your life.

FAQ

How do I start if I'm overwhelmed by debt?

Start small. Don't look at the total amount first. Just commit to looking at one statement, one bill, for five minutes. Breathe through any discomfort. Acknowledge your feelings. Then, take one tiny action: make one phone call, pay one minimum payment, or simply write down the total. The goal isn't to solve it all at once, but to break the cycle of avoidance. Small, consistent steps build momentum. Remember, your bank account is a mirror, not a scorecard. It's showing you where you are, not judging who you are.

I feel like I'm constantly chasing money. How do I stop?

This feeling often comes from a deep-seated belief in scarcity. The poverty consciousness doesn't care how much you earn; it can make you feel like you're chasing even when you have plenty. The antidote is conscious gratitude and intentional giving. Practice acknowledging the abundance you already have, however small. Give a small amount of money, time, or energy to something you care about. This shifts your internal narrative from "never enough" to "more than enough," even if your external circumstances haven't changed yet. This internal shift is critical to breaking the chase.

Is it really okay to feel emotional about money? I thought I should be logical.

Absolutely. It's not just okay; it's essential to acknowledge your emotions around money. Trying to suppress them or pretend they don't exist is like trying to drive a car with the emergency brake on. Money is deeply tied to our survival, security, and sense of worth. Of course it's emotional. The goal isn't to become purely logical - that's impossible for a human being - but to become aware of your emotions, understand their root, and learn to respond to them consciously, rather than reacting from old wounds. Notice what your body does when you think about your debt. That's not logic; that's emotion, and it needs to be honored and understood.

This journey is a marathon, not a sprint. There will be days you feel aligned, and days you fall back into old patterns. That's okay. The key is compassion, persistence, and a willingness to keep showing up for yourself. Your financial well-being is an integral part of your overall well-being. It deserves your conscious attention and your whole self.

Today, I challenge you to take one small, uncomfortable step toward greater financial awareness. Look at your bank account balance, just look at it, without judgment, for one minute. Breathe. Just one minute. That's all. And do it again tomorrow.

Om Shanti Shanti Shanti
meta_description: Develop a financial practice that aligns with your values. Understand your money story, address anxieties, and build a system that supports your well-being.
author: Kalesh
